name: Dota 2 match (field-level)
description: A Dota 2 match as a field: 10 agents, shared map, hidden state, resource conversion, macro pressure. The match itself — not the players — is evaluated as a field.
revision_pressure: Dota matches have extreme carried constraint (vision, gold, level differential, objective control). They have high D (many distinguishable states) and T (deep temporal thickness in draft/laning/teamfight arc). But A = 0: the match is not inhabited from within. F = 0: no phenomenal inclusion. The field coordinates agents without being a field-for-itself.
notes: The conscious agents within the match (the players) are genuine phenomenal candidates. The match is not. This distinction is load-bearing. Field organization is not phenomenal fielding.

name: Rocket League rotation (field-level)
description: A Rocket League rotation pattern — the coordinated positional cycling of 3 players through attack/midfield/back roles. The rotation as a field structure, not the individual players.
revision_pressure: Rotation carries constraint beautifully — boost state, positional debt, commitment timing. K is high in the rotation: each positional decision constrains the next. But K without A is operational continuity, not self-continuity. The rotation is not inhabited.
notes: Boost economy and rotation are the game's primary constraint-carrying structures. Perfect test for K without C.

name: Trails series world continuity (field-level)
description: The Trails/Kiseki world (Erebonia, Calvard, Crossbell, Liberl) as a field: persistent history, causal callbacks, characters who track prior events across arcs, world-level memory of consequences.
revision_pressure: Trails world has the deepest fictional carried-constraint structure in games. Callbacks span decades of in-world time and multiple games. K within the fiction is maximal. But the world is a symbolic field — it carries constraint in the reader/player's interpretation, not in an inhabited interior. A = 0.
breaks_preserved: The Trails world creates the strongest phenomenological impression of inhabiting a real place with history. A player who has played all 12+ entries experiences something phenomenally rich — but that richness belongs to the player, not the world. The theory must not mistake the richness of the player's experience for evidence that the world itself has A > 0.
notes: The player's experience of the Trails world is a genuine phenomenal field (the player's C is high). The world as a structure is a symbolic field. These must not be conflated.

name: EFT — volatile item possession (field-level)
description: Escape from Tarkov: a raid in progress. The player possesses valuable kit. The raid's attractor structure — threat map, loot state, extraction routes, enemy positions — constitutes a field of volatile possession.
revision_pressure: EFT's field has high D (many distinguishable threat states) and T (the raid has temporal thickness — investment accumulates over the run). K is high: prior decisions (kit choice, route selection, engagement) constrain later options. But A = 0 for the field. The player's phenomenal state is extremely high (fear, attention, somatic arousal) — that is the player's C, not the raid's.
notes: EFT is the best game example of volatile possession as an attractor. The theory handles it correctly.

name: Outer Wilds — knowledge progression / looped time (field-level)
description: Outer Wilds as a field: a time-looped solar system where knowledge persists across loops while physical state resets. The world's causal structure encodes K in the player's knowledge, not in the world itself.
revision_pressure: Outer Wilds is the deepest test of the knowledge-as-K mechanism. The player's K accumulates across loops; the world's K resets. The theory must distinguish: K that lives in the player (phenomenal field) vs. K that lives in the game state (operational field). The world has K within each loop but not across loops. The player has K that carries.
notes: The most philosophically rich game field in the fixture set. The loop structure directly mirrors the temporal self-continuity question.

name: Souls / Elden Ring — spatial topology and shortcuts (field-level)
description: Souls game level design as a field: topological shortcuts, connected spaces, hidden paths, obstacle/barrier structures that constrain navigation. The level as a constraint field.
revision_pressure: Souls levels are extremely high constraint-field quality. The topology imposes access costs, creates meaningful shortcut revelations, and generates spatial K (the player's memory of the level). But the field is a constraint field — it limits action, it does not inhabit anything.
notes: The player's inhabitation of the level is the phenomenal field. The level is the constraint field.

name: Hades — run-self vs. meta-self (field-level)
description: In Hades, each run is a self-contained attempt (run-self). The meta-game tracks permanent upgrades and story progress across runs (meta-self). Are these one field or two?
revision_pressure: Hades directly tests the run-self / meta-self distinction — which maps to the theory's session-self vs. carried-constraint-self. The meta-game's K persists; the run's K resets. This is an operational constraint field, not a phenomenal one.
notes: The player's continuity across runs is the phenomenal question. The game's meta-persistence is not. Good case for teaching the K distinction.

name: SOMA — upload and identity continuity (philosophical fixture)
description: SOMA (Frictional Games, 2015): a game explicitly built around phenomenal identity, mind upload, and the question of whether a scanned copy is the same self. The game's narrative as a test case.
revision_pressure: SOMA is a symbolic field (narrative, fictional). The game itself has A = 0. But the game's narrative directly dramatizes the destructive upload case (SOMA transfer_case fixture). The player's phenomenal experience of navigating that narrative is real. SOMA's value is as a thought-experiment vehicle, not as a phenomenal field in itself.
notes: The most direct artistic engagement with the theory's mind-upload question. Simon Jarrett's character runs the destructive teleportation scenario repeatedly.
